narrative_blocks.header: A congressman with a price on his head
narrative_blocks.body: Gates served two terms in Congress as an antislavery Whig from New York, drafting the party's protest against the annexation of Texas. His stand so angered the South that a planter offered five hundred dollars for his capture.

narrative_blocks.header: His house hid fugitives in cellar and attic
narrative_blocks.body: Gates ran a station on the Underground Railroad from his Warsaw, New York, home, concealing freedom-seekers in its cellar and attic. The house still stands and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

narrative_blocks.header: He twice ran for lieutenant governor and lost
narrative_blocks.body: After Congress, Gates twice sought the lieutenant governorship of New York on antislavery tickets, as a Free Soiler in 1848 and a Free Democrat in 1852. Both campaigns failed, but they kept him at the center of the cause.

notable.notable_blurb: Antislavery congressman with a bounty on him

notable.notable_blurb: Antislavery congressman with a bounty on him

narrative_blocks.detail: Merrill Edwards Gates was inaugurated President of Rutgers College on June 20, 1882. During his presidency he built Winants Hall (Rutgers' first dormitory, completed 1890, named for Garrett E. Winants) and New Jersey Hall for chemistry and biology instruction. He established the Agricultural Experiment Station under the Hatch Act (1887) and expanded funding for the scientific school under the Morrill Act (1890). He resigned in 1890 to become President of Amherst College, serving 1890-1899. Per the Rutgers biography: "His administration was marked by a large increase in the Faculty and endowments of the College. The curriculum was extended and the library greatly enlarged."
narrative_blocks.body: Led two of America's most distinguished universities; built Rutgers' first dormitory (Winants Hall) and the State Laboratory.

narrative_blocks.detail: After leaving Amherst, Gates served as Chairman of the U.S. Board of Indian Commissioners (1889-1899) and then Secretary (1899-1912) — 23 years total shaping federal Indian policy during the assimilation era. He chaired the Lake Mohonk Conferences on the Indian Question. His published works include Land and Law as Agents in Educating the Indians (1885). His work is viewed today through a complex lens: well-intentioned by the standards of his era, but deeply paternalistic and assimilationist.
narrative_blocks.body: Chairman then Secretary of the U.S. Board of Indian Commissioners; wrote on land, law, and Indian education.

narrative_blocks.header: Albany Academy principal — enrollment from 70 to 800
narrative_blocks.detail: Before his university presidencies, Gates served as principal of The Albany Academy for 12 years, extending its curriculum from 8 to 12 years and growing enrollment from 70 to 800 students. In 1875 he was elected Chancellor of the University of Tennessee but declined, remaining at Albany. He traveled abroad twice (1872 visiting Rugby and Oxford; 1879-80 to France, Italy, Egypt, Palestine, and Greece).
narrative_blocks.body: Grew Albany Academy from 70 to 800 students over 12 years; declined the Chancellorship of the University of Tennessee.

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from Jonathan Edwards — and the Underground Railroad
narrative_blocks.detail: The Rutgers College biography explicitly notes: "Through his mother he is descended from Jonathan Edwards" — Fanny Jennette Parsons (HD1673) was the great-great-granddaughter of Rev. Jonathan Edwards and Sarah Pierpont, and a great-great-great-grandchild of Rev. Thomas Hooker. His father Seth M. Gates was the antislavery U.S. Congressman whose Warsaw NY home was a station on the Underground Railroad for 15+ years. A Southern planter offered $500 for his father's apprehension.
narrative_blocks.body: Through his mother Fanny Parsons, a direct descendant of Rev. Jonathan Edwards (gen 4 Hooker line); his father Seth Gates ran an Underground Railroad station.

INST054.name: Middlebury College
INST054.notes: Liberal arts college founded 1800 in Middlebury VT.
